evidence.evidence_text:
The user wants to identify an object held by the main female tennis player, other than a tennis ball and racket.
I need to scan the provided images for the main character (the female tennis player) and see if she is holding any of the listed options: water bottle, wristband, sunglasses, towel, energy bar.

1.  **Analyze the images:**
    *   **Image 1 (6s):** Shows three girls. The girl in the middle (blonde ponytail) is facing away. The girl on the right (blonde ponytail) is facing right. The girl on the left is facing right. No clear holding of specific objects like a water bottle or towel is obvious here, though the girl on the right might be holding something near her shoulder, it's hard to tell. Wait, looking closely at Image 1, the girl on the right has a blue strap, maybe a bag. The girl in the middle has a white shirt.
    *   **Image 2 (7s):** A female tennis player in a white tank top and dark skirt is serving. She is holding a racket. A tennis ball is in the air. No other object.
    *   **Image 3 (8s):** The same player is preparing to hit. Holding a racket.
    *   **Image 4 (9s):** The player is celebrating or reacting. Holding a racket in her left hand. Her right hand is clenched in a fist. No other object.
    *   **Image 5 (10s):** The player is holding a racket with both hands, looking down.
    *   **Image 6 (60s):** A group of people standing near a fence. A girl in a white shirt is drinking from a white water bottle. This is a different scene. Is this the "main character"? The prompt asks about the "main female tennis player". The earlier frames (2-5) show a player in action. The later frames (6-8) show a group. Let's look at the group.
    *   **Image 7 (61s):** A boy with a racket walks past. The girl in the white shirt is talking.
    *   **Image 8 (62s):** The group is walking.

2.  **Evaluate the "Main Character":**
    *   The prompt implies a single main character. Frames 2-5 clearly show a tennis player in action. Frames 6-8 show a group

transcript_spans.text: It's the greatest feeling. Just seeing these kids kind of latch on to a sport that I love so much and just the smiles on their face when they kind of, you know, hit a forehand for the first time or, you know, something just clicks in their head where they're like, oh my god, I know how to do this. It's just a really amazing feeling to know that I have an impact on their lives and that I get to show them something that I love so much and see them love it just as much.

Champlin Park bowed out early in the section tournament, but with a pretty young squad, Replier believes the future is bright for Rebels tennis. All of these girls work so hard in the offseason, and we all play constantly almost every day in the offseason. And so as we look into the future, we know that even if this year isn't perfect, we have time to grow, and we can do even better next year and just keep working hard. Jason Melillo, CCX Sports.
Find more prep sports games and highlights at ccxmedia.org and follow us on social media.

transcript_spans.text: Alex Replier calls the tennis court her happy place. The Chamberlain Park Junior tried a lot of sports growing up, but tennis stuck. It was just something about the fact that when I'm on the court, it's me and I get to make decisions. And if you know I messed up, it's me. And if I win, it's me.

Replier started playing when she was five years old and made the CP varsity as a seventh grader. In ninth grade, she became the Rebels' number one singles player. I went from just being the flattest, hardest hitter ever to someone who I can actually hit topspin now and just really, I know how to construct points better and I know how to really just set up a strategy that'll work to have me be successful on the court.

Replier is a team captain this season and tries to be a leader and mentor to younger players. She really took me under her wing. She just made sure I felt like I could talk to her about anything. And if I had any questions, I could go to her. Year after year, she got a little more mature. She started to make friends. And she's really blossomed into the leader of Temple Park tennis.

transcript_spans.text: Replier is always striving to get better and to that end she plays on the club circuit as well. I love competing in tournaments whether they're local level or national level and the club environment is just so fun and it is something I really love to do is just compete and every weekend you know we're traveling to different cities and spending a lot of time on the court.

Still, Replier says the club tennis world is very different from high school and can sometimes be difficult to manage. I've kind of built a community with my family and with my coaches where, you know, I have a supportive group around me, but definitely when you're on the court, you're just kind of sitting on the bench yourself. It definitely can get a little, feel a little lonely. When she's not playing tennis or keeping up with her schoolwork, Alex Replier is probably teaching the game. She volunteers at Inner City Tennis in Minneapolis.

transcript_segments.text: It's the greatest feeling. Just seeing these kids kind of latch on to a sport that I love so much and just the smiles on their face when they kind of, you know, hit a forehand for the first time or, you know, something just clicks in their head where they're like, oh my god, I know how to do this. It's just a really amazing feeling to know that I have an impact on their lives and that I get to show them something that I love so much and see them love it just as much.

Champlin Park bowed out early in the section tournament, but with a pretty young squad, Replier believes the future is bright for Rebels tennis. All of these girls work so hard in the offseason, and we all play constantly almost every day in the offseason. And so as we look into the future, we know that even if this year isn't perfect, we have time to grow, and we can do even better next year and just keep working hard. Jason Melillo, CCX Sports.
Find more prep sports games and highlights at ccxmedia.org and follow us on social media.

transcript_segments.text: Alex Replier calls the tennis court her happy place. The Chamberlain Park Junior tried a lot of sports growing up, but tennis stuck. It was just something about the fact that when I'm on the court, it's me and I get to make decisions. And if you know I messed up, it's me. And if I win, it's me.

Replier started playing when she was five years old and made the CP varsity as a seventh grader. In ninth grade, she became the Rebels' number one singles player. I went from just being the flattest, hardest hitter ever to someone who I can actually hit topspin now and just really, I know how to construct points better and I know how to really just set up a strategy that'll work to have me be successful on the court.

Replier is a team captain this season and tries to be a leader and mentor to younger players. She really took me under her wing. She just made sure I felt like I could talk to her about anything. And if I had any questions, I could go to her. Year after year, she got a little more mature. She started to make friends. And she's really blossomed into the leader of Temple Park tennis.

prior_trace_claims.text: In the main player's action frames, she is shown holding a racket, and at 7s a tennis ball is visible, but no other object is clearly shown in her hand. This does not support sunglasses, a towel, a wristband, or an energy bar being briefly held by the main player in the retrieved frames.

A water bottle is the only answer option that is directly observed being held in the retrieved frames, but that observation is in the later group scene and is not securely tied to the main player. Because the question asks what the main character holds briefly, the available direct visual evidence does not uniquely support any option for the main player.

The main character is best grounded by the action sequence at 7s-10s, where one female tennis player is centered and repeatedly shown playing tennis. The later 60s-62s frames are a separate group scene rather than the same clearly centered action subject.
